_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: He was handed to a broken country overnight, and by morning the shelves were full again.

museum_objects.title: 20 Deutsche Mark Banknote (1948)
museum_objects.description: An original 20 DM note from the June 1948 currency reform that replaced the worthless Reichsmark overnight.
museum_objects.significance: Overnight, an entire country's shattered economy was handed a fresh start in the form of this note — shelves that had been empty for years filled up within days.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of the very first Deutsche Mark bills, handed out in 1948 to instantly replace Germany's worthless wartime money.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so. | IMAGE QA FAIL: the image shows a 2 DM note, not a 20 DM note as claimed

museum_objects.title: 'Bird Dog' Wooden Currency Transport Crate
museum_objects.description: One of only three surviving wooden crates used in the secret U.S. military operation to transport the new Deutsche Mark currency into occupied Germany.
museum_objects.significance: Klaus arrived in secrecy, smuggled in under a codename, inside crates like this one — proof that rebuilding trust sometimes has to start as a covert operation.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of only three surviving wooden crates from the secret U.S. military mission that smuggled new German money into the country in 1948.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Operation Bird Dog Archive
museum_objects.description: TIME magazine's contemporary coverage of the covert American operation to introduce the new Deutsche Mark.
museum_objects.significance: The American press called it 'Operation Bird Dog' — a codename for the exact moment Klaus was smuggled into existence.
museum_objects.plain_language: An old TIME magazine article covering the secret U.S. operation that introduced Germany's new currency in 1948.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Trümmerfrauen ('Rubble Women') Photographs
museum_objects.description: Photographs documenting the German women who cleared war rubble by hand in the years before and during the currency reform.
museum_objects.significance: This is the Germany Klaus was born into — physically shattered, rebuilt one brick at a time by hand, right before his arrival gave that labor real economic meaning.
museum_objects.plain_language: Photos of German women clearing war rubble by hand after World War II, right before the new currency arrived to rebuild the economy.
museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Bundesbank Geldmuseum DM Banknotes and Coins Archive
museum_objects.description: The Deutsche Bundesbank's comprehensive archive of every Deutsche Mark note and coin ever issued.
museum_objects.significance: Every single design change across five decades of Klaus's life is documented here, down to the smallest security thread.
museum_objects.plain_language: Germany's central bank's complete archive of every Deutsche Mark bill and coin ever made.

world_map.map_description: Klaus was smuggled into a broken postwar Germany in secret military crates and became the anchor currency of Europe's strongest economy before retiring for the euro.

nodes.label: Frankfurt (Reichsbank Building)
nodes.description: Site of the old Reichsbank building used for the 1948 currency distribution across occupied Germany.

nodes.label: Berlin (Airlift/Blockade)
nodes.description: The Soviet blockade of Berlin began just days after the currency reform, tying Klaus's birth directly to Cold War tensions.

nodes.label: Bremerhaven
nodes.description: The port where the secret 'Bird Dog' currency shipment crates landed from the United States.

nodes.label: Bad Homburg
nodes.description: Location where one of the three surviving 'Bird Dog' transport crates is preserved today.

nodes.label: Frankfurt (ECB)
nodes.description: Where Klaus's story ended in 2002 as the Deutsche Mark was retired for the euro.

historical_map_source.title: Bundesbank — The Economic and Currency Reform of 1948
